---
title: "mars() Function Documentation"
subtitle: "STAT 360 Final Project"
authors: "Akaaljot Mathoda (SFU ID: XXXXXXXX), Mayank Mayank (301563549)"
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output: html_document
---

**GitHub Repository:** https://github.com/Jassa47/STAT360-MARS-Project

---

## Description

`mars()` fits a **Multivariate Adaptive Regression Splines (MARS)** model.
MARS is a flexible nonlinear regression method that automatically discovers
piecewise linear relationships and interactions between predictors using
hinge basis functions.

---

## Usage
```r
mars(formula, data, control = mars.control())

mars.control(Mmax = 2, d = 3, trace = FALSE)
```

---

## Arguments

**`mars()`**

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `formula` | An R formula of the form `response ~ predictors` |
| `data` | A data frame containing all variables in `formula` |
| `control` | A `mars.control` object. Default is `mars.control()` |

**`mars.control()`**

| Argument | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| `Mmax` | Maximum number of basis functions in the forward pass. Must be a positive even integer. Default is 2 |
| `d` | Penalty per effective parameter in the GCV criterion. Default is 3 |
| `trace` | Logical. Print forward pass progress? Default FALSE |

---

## Details

MARS builds a regression model of the form:
$$\hat{f}(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{m=0}^{M} \beta_m B_m(\mathbf{x})$$
where each $B_m$ is a product of hinge functions and $\beta_m$ are
coefficients estimated by ordinary least squares.

### Hinge Functions

The fundamental building block is:
$$[s \cdot (x_v - t)]_+ = \max(0,\ s \cdot (x_v - t))$$
where $s \in \{+1, -1\}$ is the sign, $x_v$ is a predictor, and $t$ is
the knot location. These are always added in reflected pairs.

### Forward Pass (Algorithm 2, Friedman 1991)

Starting from the constant basis function $B_0 = 1$, the forward pass
greedily adds pairs of hinge functions by searching over all combinations
of existing basis function $m$, predictor variable $v$, and knot location
$t$, selecting the combination that minimises the GCV criterion.

### GCV Criterion

$$\text{GCV}(M) = \frac{RSS/N}{\left(1 - \tilde{C}(M)/N\right)^2}$$

where $\tilde{C}(M) = \hat{C}(M) + d \cdot M$, $\hat{C}(M) = \sum \text{hat-values}$,
and $d = 3$ penalises model complexity (Friedman 1991, eq. 32).

### Backward Pass (Algorithm 3, Friedman 1991)

Starting from the full forward model, basis functions are removed one at
a time. The removal that degrades GCV the least is chosen at each step.
The globally best model across all sizes is returned. The intercept basis
function $B_0$ is never removed.

### Final Model
```r
lm(y ~ . - 1, data = data.frame(y = y, bwd$B))
```

The output inherits from class `lm`, so `residuals()`, `fitted()`,
`coef()` and other `lm` methods work directly.

---

## Value

`mars()` returns an S3 object of class `c("mars", "lm")` containing:

| Component | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `call` | The matched function call |
| `formula` | The formula supplied by the user |
| `y` | The numeric response vector |
| `B` | Data frame of selected basis functions (columns B0, B1, ...) |
| `Bfuncs` | List describing each basis function. `Bfuncs[[1]]` is `NULL` (intercept). Others are matrices with columns `s` (sign), `v` (variable index), `t` (knot) |
| `x_names` | Character vector of predictor variable names |
| `coefficients` | Named vector of fitted coefficients |
| `...` | All other standard `lm` output components |

`mars.control()` returns a named list of class `"mars.control"` with
components `Mmax`, `d`, and `trace`.

---

## Author(s)

Akaaljot Mathoda \<your\@sfu.ca\>

---

## References

Friedman, J.H. (1991). Multivariate Adaptive Regression Splines (with
discussion). *The Annals of Statistics*, **19**(1), 1--141.

Hastie, T., Tibshirani, R., and Friedman, J. (2009). *The Elements of
Statistical Learning*, 2nd ed. Springer. Chapter 9.

---

## See Also

Methods for `mars` objects:

- `print.mars()` — displays call and coefficients
- `summary.mars()` — prints basis function descriptions and full lm summary
- `plot.mars()` — 2D plots for single-variable basis functions; 3D `persp()` plots for two-variable basis functions
- `predict.mars()` — predictions for new data via `make_B()`
- `anova.mars()` — analysis of variance table

Also: `mars.control()` for control objects; `h()` for the hinge function.

---

## Examples

### Example 1: marstestdata
```r
library(mars)
load("tests/testthat/marstestdata.rda")
mc  <- mars.control(Mmax = 10, d = 3, trace = FALSE)
fit <- mars(y ~ ., data = marstestdata, control = mc)
print(fit)
summary(fit)
plot(fit)
predict(fit, newdata = head(marstestdata, 5))
anova(fit)
```

### Example 2: Boston Housing Data
```r
library(MASS)
data("Boston")
fit2 <- mars(medv ~ ., data = Boston, control = mars.control(Mmax = 20))
print(fit2)
summary(fit2)
predict(fit2, newdata = head(Boston, 5))
anova(fit2)
```

### Example 3: Simulated Nonlinear Data with Train/Test Split
```r
set.seed(42)
n  <- 200
x1 <- runif(n, -2, 2); x2 <- runif(n, -2, 2); x3 <- runif(n, -2, 2)
y  <- sin(pi*x1) + (x2 - 0.5)^2 + abs(x3) + rnorm(n, sd = 0.3)
dat <- data.frame(y, x1, x2, x3)

set.seed(7)
idx   <- sample(n, 160)
train <- dat[idx, ]; test <- dat[-idx, ]

fit3  <- mars(y ~ x1 + x2 + x3, data = train,
              control = mars.control(Mmax = 12))
print(fit3)
summary(fit3)
preds <- predict(fit3, newdata = test)
cat("Test RMSE:", sqrt(mean((test$y - preds)^2)), "\n")
anova(fit3)
```
